clean_story:
Boundaries between states. Crossings that transform.

The word threshold comes from Old English þrescold, meaning the wooden sill under a door—a place where stepping over changes everything.

Thresholds feel sacred because they force a choice: enter or stay. Like a riverbank dividing land from water, they mark where one set of rules ends and another begins. The act of crossing isn’t neutral—it alters identity. A graduate walking across a stage isn’t just moving; they’re becoming someone new. A cell’s membrane isn’t just a wall; it decides what enters life itself.

This power comes from precision. Thresholds are binary: pregnant/not pregnant, asleep/awake, alive/dead. No middle. Rituals exploit this—weddings, funerals, initiations—because ambiguity terrifies us. Even machines rely on thresholds: a thermostat’s click at 68°F isn’t random; it’s the exact point where "cold" becomes "warm."

Three related subjects are liminal spaces, rites of passage, and how cells use gates.
